news_summary: Efforts are being made to preserve Nepal’s medicinal plants and Indigenous knowledge, ensuring the protection and continuation of valuable cultural and natural heritage for future generations.
news_summary_fr: Des efforts sont déployés pour préserver les plantes médicinales et les savoirs autochtones du Népal, afin de garantir la protection et la pérennité d'un patrimoine culturel et naturel précieux pour les générations futures.
news_summary_es: Se están realizando esfuerzos para preservar las plantas medicinales y los conocimientos indígenas de Nepal, con el fin de garantizar la protección y la perpetuación de un valioso patrimonio cultural y natural para las generaciones futuras.

articles.title: Archiving Nepal’s medicinal plants and Indigenous knowledge for future generations
articles.summary: Sanjib Chaudhary, a Nepalese writer and archivist, documents healers’ medicinal plant knowledge in audio and video, ensuring future generations access Indigenous ecological wisdom rooted in community consent and reusable media.
articles.content:
Most of the Tharu community’s medicinal and cultural knowledge in the western and southern plains of Nepal and northern India has been passed down orally, from healer to healer and generation to generation. Yet this living knowledge rarely appears in archives in ways that center community voices and consent.

The OpenSpeaks Archives, launched in 2024 and hosted by the Wikimedia Foundation, is a digital language archive that supports community-based archivists in documenting, transcribing, and publishing Indigenous and low-resourced languages from India, Nepal, and Sri Lanka. As part of this work, Sanjib Chaudhary has been documenting his community’s traditional healing practices and folk knowledge through audio-visual recordings. He is a long-time Global Voices contributor, author, and Eastern (Saptariya) Tharu language activist.

This interview was conducted by Subhashish Panigrahi on behalf of Rising Voices and OpenSpeaks Archives via online call.

Rising Voices (RV): You are both documenting and archiving your community’s traditional knowledge. Could you briefly describe the project and what you hope it will achieve?

Sanjib Chaudhary (SC): As part of this project, I am interviewing Tharu traditional healers and documenting their practices in Eastern (Saptariya) Tharu. I have started with two healers from Saptari District in Madhesh Province, eastern Nepal, and I plan to reach more healers in Dang District in western Nepal and in Chitwan District in central Nepal. I want people to see how Tharu healing systems work across this east — west geography — how the plants, remedies, and rituals change from place to place, and what they have in common. These healers prepare many important decoctions and concoctions from local medicinal plants. I hope the documentation will be useful not only to my community but also to those working in Ayurveda and other traditional systems of medicine.

RV: Why did you choose audio-visual documentation instead of purely text-based documentation?

SC: Earlier, through another grant, I had written about Tharu traditional healers in text form, but I increasingly felt that it was not enough. When we write, we lose so much: tone, rhythm, body language, and the emotion in the healer’s voice. Many people also do not have the time or habit to read long articles; they skim. With audio and video, they can hear the healer’s voice directly instead of reading my description of them. That creates trust and credibility — people see that these are real community members speaking about their own practices, not someone speaking on their behalf.

RV: Today, many people also upload oral histories and interviews to YouTube. How is your work different from simply putting videos on YouTube?

SC: YouTube is important because many people use it, but in its current form it adds more credibility. We follow specific documentation protocols; the documentation is directly from the field and is not for commercial use but for researchers, educators, and community members. This infrastructure gives the content a different kind of credibility and longevity.

RV: How can the tools and frameworks we’ve created for subtitling, documentation workflows, and language documentation guides be useful for you and other Tharu community-based documenters? How do you plan to use them?

SC: They are very useful for community-based documenters. I am not only creating new recordings. I am also integrating them into various Wikipedia articles, enriching them with authentic audio-visual evidence from the community. This improves both the depth and credibility of those articles. I also contribute those materials to Wikisource and Wikidata. Subtitling in Eastern Tharu, Nepali, and English makes the content accessible to my own community as well as to people who do not speak Tharu but are interested in the knowledge. I call this the “media multiplier” effect—a single recording on one platform multiplies its impact across several platforms.

RV: Wikimedia projects usually avoid “original research.” Yet oral documentation like yours often contains primary knowledge that is not written anywhere else. How do you see the value of these recordings in relation to academic citation and GLAM collaborations?

SC: Often, when audio or video is uploaded to Wikimedia Commons, it only says this is the language spoken in this clip. But the actual knowledge spoken in the clip matters. I hope we archive the material properly and credit the documenters, so that future writers and researchers can use and cite such knowledge. If community members know their work will be cited and recognized, they are more likely to spend time documenting, transcribing, and annotating. It also means that the community’s own interpretations and explanations enter the record, not just the interpretations of external researchers.

RV: You mentioned concerns about how community knowledge is used. What kind of safeguards or recognition are needed to document and share the Tharu community’s knowledge?

SC: When we publish deep community knowledge—especially about medicinal practices, rituals, or other sensitive areas—we have to think carefully about where and how it is stored. The knowledge belongs to the community as a whole. Ideally, the documentation should clearly state that this is Tharu community knowledge, recorded with consent, and that the community retains rights over reuses. If someone in the future uses our recordings, they should acknowledge that the material comes from the Tharu community and from specific healers, and follow the permissions the community has given.

RV: Looking ahead, what do you see as the long-term benefits of this documentation for the Tharu community itself?
articles.reasoning: The article describes a meaningful initiative to preserve and document Indigenous medicinal knowledge of the Tharu community in Nepal through audio-visual archives with community consent. This project has broad cultural and societal significance by safeguarding endangered traditional knowledge, empowering the community, and providing valuable resources for education, research, and healthcare. The use of digital archives with proper attribution and consent ensures respect for Indigenous rights and promotes knowledge accessibility and longevity, fulfilling criteria for positive real-world impact, significance, and substance.
articles.personality_title: Recording Nepal’s Tharu healers to preserve traditional medicine
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Tharu community in Nepal and northern India has passed down knowledge about medicinal plants and healing by talking from one generation to the next. This knowledge is rarely written down or saved in ways that respect the community’s voice and permission.

**What happened** – Sanjib Chaudhary, a writer and language activist from the Tharu community, started recording healers in eastern Nepal using audio and video. He interviews them about their healing practices and the plants they use. His recordings are saved in the OpenSpeaks Archives, a digital project supported by the Wikimedia Foundation that helps preserve Indigenous languages and knowledge.

**Impact** – Using audio and video lets people hear the healers directly, including their tone and emotions, which words alone cannot show. This builds trust and makes the knowledge more real and lasting. The recordings are shared with clear community consent and rules about use, so the Tharu community keeps control. The project also adds these recordings to Wikipedia and related sites with subtitles in Tharu, Nepali, and English, making the knowledge easy to find and understand.

**What's next step** – Sanjib plans to interview more healers from different parts of Nepal to show how healing practices vary across the region. The recordings will continue to be integrated into online resources and used by researchers, educators, and community members. The project encourages others in the community to document and share their knowledge safely and respectfully.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A Nepalese archivist is preserving the Tharu community’s traditional healing knowledge through trusted audio-visual recordings that respect community rights and improve access for future generations.
articles.personality_title_fr: Enregistrer les guérisseurs Tharu du Népal pour préserver la médecine traditionnelle
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La communauté Tharu au Népal et dans le nord de l’Inde transmet oralement de génération en génération ses connaissances sur les plantes médicinales et les pratiques de guérison. Ces savoirs sont rarement écrits ou archivés en respectant la voix et le consentement de la communauté.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Sanjib Chaudhary, écrivain et militant linguistique Tharu, a commencé à enregistrer en audio et vidéo des guérisseurs du Népal oriental. Il les interviewe sur leurs pratiques de guérison et les plantes qu’ils utilisent. Ses enregistrements sont conservés dans OpenSpeaks Archives, un projet numérique soutenu par la Wikimedia Foundation qui aide à préserver les langues et savoirs autochtones.

**Impact** – Utiliser l’audio et la vidéo permet d’entendre directement les guérisseurs, avec leur ton et leurs émotions, ce que les mots seuls ne peuvent transmettre. Cela crée de la confiance et rend le savoir plus réel et durable. Les enregistrements sont partagés avec le consentement clair de la communauté et des règles d’utilisation, ce qui garantit que la communauté Tharu garde le contrôle. Le projet intègre aussi ces enregistrements dans Wikipédia et sites associés, avec sous-titres en Tharu, népalais et anglais, facilitant l’accès et la compréhension.

**Prochaines étapes** – Sanjib prévoit d’interviewer d’autres guérisseurs dans différentes régions du Népal pour montrer comment les pratiques changent selon les lieux. Les enregistrements continueront d’être intégrés aux ressources en ligne et utilisés par chercheurs, enseignants et membres de la communauté. Le projet encourage aussi d’autres membres à documenter et partager leurs savoirs de manière sûre et respectueuse.

**Phrase clé** – Un archiviste népalais préserve les savoirs traditionnels de guérison de la communauté Tharu grâce à des enregistrements audio-visuels respectant les droits communautaires et facilitant l’accès pour les générations futures.
articles.personality_title_es: Grabando a los curanderos Tharu de Nepal para preservar la medicina tradicional
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La comunidad Tharu en Nepal y el norte de India ha transmitido de forma oral sus conocimientos sobre plantas medicinales y prácticas de curación de generación en generación. Estos saberes rara vez se escriben o archivan respetando la voz y el consentimiento de la comunidad.

**Qué pasó** – Sanjib Chaudhary, escritor y activista lingüístico Tharu, empezó a grabar en audio y video a curanderos del este de Nepal. Los entrevista sobre sus prácticas de curación y las plantas que usan. Sus grabaciones se guardan en OpenSpeaks Archives, un proyecto digital apoyado por Wikimedia Foundation que ayuda a preservar lenguas y conocimientos indígenas.

**Impacto** – Usar audio y video permite escuchar a los curanderos directamente, con su tono y emociones, que las palabras no pueden mostrar. Esto genera confianza y hace que el conocimiento sea más real y duradero. Las grabaciones se comparten con claro consentimiento comunitario y reglas de uso, para que la comunidad Tharu mantenga el control. El proyecto también integra estas grabaciones en Wikipedia y sitios relacionados, con subtítulos en Tharu, nepalí e inglés, facilitando el acceso y comprensión.

**Próximo paso** – Sanjib planea entrevistar a más curanderos de diferentes partes de Nepal para mostrar cómo varían las prácticas según la región. Las grabaciones seguirán integrándose en recursos en línea y usándose por investigadores, educadores y miembros de la comunidad. El proyecto anima a otros a documentar y compartir sus saberes de forma segura y respetuosa.

**Frase clave** – Un archivista nepalés está preservando el conocimiento tradicional de curación de la comunidad Tharu mediante grabaciones audiovisuales que respetan los derechos comunitarios y mejoran el acceso para futuras generaciones.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of intertwined traditional medicinal plants native to Nepal’s Tharu region, with softly glowing audio waveforms gently flowing around them, symbolizing the oral transmission of healing knowledge, set against a simple, natural-toned background evoking a serene community landscape without any human figures.
